[Clark University](/wiki/Clark_University "Clark University"), [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University"), [Brandeis University](/wiki/Brandeis_University "Brandeis University")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

(born September 8, 1951)[[1]](#cite_note-2025mediaguide-1) is an American businessman, [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") producer, and, since 1994, owner of the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). The Eagles have won two [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowls "Super Bowls") under his tenure as owner.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American businessman and sports team owner (born 1951)

| Jeffrey Lurie | |
| --- | --- |

| Alma mater | [Clark University](/wiki/Clark_University "Clark University"), [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University"), [Brandeis University](/wiki/Brandeis_University "Brandeis University")[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] |
| Organization(s) | Screen Pass Productions;[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") |

| Parent(s) | Nancy Smith Lurie Morris John Lurie |
| Family | [Philip Smith](/wiki/Philip_Smith_(theater_owner) "Philip Smith (theater owner)") (grandfather) [Richard A. Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(businessman) "Richard A. Smith (businessman)") (uncle) |
| Awards | Two-time [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") champion |

**Jeffrey Robert Lurie** (born September 8, 1951)[[1]](#cite_note-2025mediaguide-1) is an American businessman, [documentary film](/wiki/Documentary_film "Documentary film") producer, and, since 1994, owner of the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL). The Eagles have won two [Super Bowls](/wiki/Super_Bowls "Super Bowls") under his tenure as owner.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jeffrey_Lurie&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section of a [biography of a living person](/wiki/Wikipedia:Biographies_of_living_persons "Wikipedia:Biographies of living persons") **needs additional [citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help by adding [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources"). [Contentious material](/wiki/Wikipedia:Biographies_of_living_persons#Remove_contentious_material_that_is_unsourced_or_poorly_sourced "Wikipedia:Biographies of living persons") about living persons that is unsourced or [poorly sourced](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources#Questionable_and_self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources") **must be removed immediately** from the article and its talk page, especially if potentially [libelous](/wiki/Wikipedia:Libel "Wikipedia:Libel"). *Find sources:* ["Jeffrey Lurie"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Born Jeffrey Robert Lurie in [Boston, Massachusetts](/wiki/Boston,_Massachusetts "Boston, Massachusetts") on September 8, 1951,[[1]](#cite_note-2025mediaguide-1) to an American [Jewish](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews") family,[[4]](#cite_note-4)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*][[5]](#cite_note-5) Lurie is the son of Nancy (née Smith)[[6]](#cite_note-Forbes-6) and Morris John Lurie.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] His grandfather, [Philip Smith](/wiki/Philip_Smith_(theater_owner) "Philip Smith (theater owner)"), founded the [General Cinema](/wiki/General_Cinema "General Cinema") [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") chain, an early large operator of U.S. [drive-in movie theaters](/wiki/Drive-in_movie_theaters "Drive-in movie theaters").[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) He has two siblings, Peter and Cathy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] His uncle is [Richard A. Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(businessman) "Richard A. Smith (businessman)").[[6]](#cite_note-Forbes-6)

Lurie attended [Browne and Nichols School](/wiki/Buckingham_Browne_%26_Nichols_School "Buckingham Browne & Nichols School") in [Cambridge, Massachusetts](/wiki/Cambridge,_Massachusetts "Cambridge, Massachusetts").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Lurie's father died on April 14, 1961, at the age of 44; Jeffrey was nine years old.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Lurie went on to earn a [B.A.](/wiki/B.A. "B.A.") from [Clark University](/wiki/Clark_University "Clark University"),[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and a master's degree in psychology from [Boston University](/wiki/Boston_University "Boston University").[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He then earned a doctorate in social policy from [The Heller School for Social Policy and Management](/wiki/Heller_School_for_Social_Policy_and_Management "Heller School for Social Policy and Management") at [Brandeis University](/wiki/Brandeis_University "Brandeis University"),[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] where he wrote his thesis on the depiction of women in Hollywood movies.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jeffrey_Lurie&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Jeffrey_Lurie_and_Tina_Lai_Is_This_Thing_On-60.jpg/250px-Jeffrey_Lurie_and_Tina_Lai_Is_This_Thing_On-60.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jeffrey_Lurie_and_Tina_Lai_Is_This_Thing_On-60.jpg)

Jeffrey Lurie and Tina Lai at the 2025 New York Film Festival for Is This Thing On?

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This section of a [biography of a living person](/wiki/Wikipedia:Biographies_of_living_persons "Wikipedia:Biographies of living persons") **needs additional [citations](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help by adding [reliable sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources"). [Contentious material](/wiki/Wikipedia:Biographies_of_living_persons#Remove_contentious_material_that_is_unsourced_or_poorly_sourced "Wikipedia:Biographies of living persons") about living persons that is unsourced or [poorly sourced](/wiki/Wikipedia:Reliable_sources#Questionable_and_self-published_sources "Wikipedia:Reliable sources") **must be removed immediately** from the article and its talk page, especially if potentially [libelous](/wiki/Wikipedia:Libel "Wikipedia:Libel"). *Find sources:* ["Jeffrey Lurie"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Jeffrey+Lurie%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(September 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Forbes has described Lurie as beginning "his professional career as a social policy teacher at Boston University";[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) specifically, he served as an adjunct assistant professor of social policy at Boston University.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1983, Lurie left academia to join [General Cinema](/wiki/General_Cinema "General Cinema") Corporation,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] the conglomerate that began as an early [movie theater](/wiki/Movie_theater "Movie theater") chain (founded by his grandfather, [Philip Smith](/wiki/Philip_Smith_(theater_owner) "Philip Smith (theater owner)")).[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2)[[7]](#cite_note-7) At the time he joined, General Cinema was headed by his uncle, [Richard A. Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(businessman) "Richard A. Smith (businessman)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] At General Cinema, Lurie worked as an executive in the company as liaison between General Cinema and the production community in Hollywood, and as an advisor in the company's national film buying office.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1984, General Cinema acquired a minority interest in [Carter Hawley Hale](/wiki/Carter_Hawley_Hale "Carter Hawley Hale"), at the time the tenth-largest clothing retailer in the United States, whose holdings included [Bergdorf Goodman](/wiki/Bergdorf_Goodman "Bergdorf Goodman") and [Neiman-Marcus](/wiki/Neiman-Marcus "Neiman-Marcus").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*] In 1993, its holding company, by then named [Harcourt General](/wiki/Harcourt_(publisher) "Harcourt (publisher)"), spun-off General Cinema as a separate company.[[8]](#cite_note-8) After competition forced the closure of a number of General Cinema locations,[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) General Cinema filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy on October 11, 2000, with accompanying resignation of [Richard A. Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(businessman) "Richard A. Smith (businessman)") and a further employed Smith family member.[[11]](#cite_note-11) On December 7, 2001, [AMC Theatres](/wiki/AMC_Theatres "AMC Theatres") agreed to buy GC Companies, Inc. as part of GC's Chapter 11 reorganization plan, an acquisition completed on March 29, 2002.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) Lurie departed General Cinema.[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*][*[verification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

By 1985, Lurie had also created his first film company, Chestnut Hill Productions; through it, he produced a number of feature film releases, "not necessarily considered artistic masterpieces", including *[Foxfire](/wiki/Foxfire_(1996_film) "Foxfire (1996 film)")* (1996), starring [Angelina Jolie](/wiki/Angelina_Jolie "Angelina Jolie").[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) The company also produced television commercials.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Chestnut Hill Productions has since produced a string of Hollywood movies and TV shows, initially starting off with a multi-feature joint venture with [Tri-Star Pictures](/wiki/TriStar_Pictures "TriStar Pictures") to develop projects.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1987, three Chestnut Hill projects were picked up by [MGM/UA Communications Co.](/wiki/Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer "Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] two each by [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures") and [Warner Bros. Pictures](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Pictures "Warner Bros. Pictures"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and one by [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures");[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] a majority of the eight Chestnut Hill productions would be received[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] by [Tri-Star Pictures](/wiki/TriStar_Pictures "TriStar Pictures"), which then became an independent production company.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] ([Tri-Star Pictures](/wiki/TriStar_Pictures "TriStar Pictures") received first look at all projects handled by Chestnut Hill Productions, with Tri-Star and Chestnut Hill co-financing development and Tri-Star picking up the cost of production.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[13]](#cite_note-13)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*])

Lurie's wife, Christina, has joined him as a producer in his film work,[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] which as of 2011 was operating as Screen Pass Productions.[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) On February 27, 2011, the movie *[Inside Job](/wiki/Inside_Job_(2010_film) "Inside Job (2010 film)")* (2010), produced by the Luries and detailing the [financial crisis of 2008](/wiki/Financial_crisis_of_2008 "Financial crisis of 2008"), won an Academy Award (Oscar) for best documentary film;[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2)[[14]](#cite_note-14)[*[better source needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Questionable_sources "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

> In a released statement, the Luries said they were “humbled” by the award.[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2)

Lurie was 59 years of age, at the time.[[2]](#cite_note-BurkeForbes-2) Two years later, Lurie won a second Oscar when *[Inocente](/wiki/Inocente "Inocente")*, a film that he executive produced, won for Best Documentary Short Film.[[15]](#cite_note-JLAA-15)

### Philadelphia Eagles ownership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jeffrey_Lurie&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Philadelphia Eagles ownership")]

As a fan of all the Boston sports teams, Lurie went to games and put himself to sleep listening to the [Boston Red Sox](/wiki/Boston_Red_Sox "Boston Red Sox") on his transistor radio.[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] The Luries had been [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") season-ticket holders since the franchise began in 1960, the year the [American Football League](/wiki/American_Football_League "American Football League") was founded.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Lurie cheered for [Gino Cappelletti](/wiki/Gino_Cappelletti "Gino Cappelletti"), [Houston Antwine](/wiki/Houston_Antwine "Houston Antwine"), and [Babe Parilli](/wiki/Babe_Parilli "Babe Parilli").[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] In 1993, Lurie tried to buy the Patriots, but he dropped out of the bidding at $150 million.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] (His uncle [Richard Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(businessman) "Richard A. Smith (businessman)") nixed the purchase based on the financials.[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*]) Lurie's name also had surfaced in sale talks regarding the [Los Angeles Rams](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Rams "Los Angeles Rams"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and he was a potential investor in a bid for a Baltimore expansion team with [Robert Tisch](/wiki/Robert_Tisch "Robert Tisch"), who subsequently bought 50% of the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants").[*[according to whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*]

Five months later, [Richard Smith](/wiki/Richard_A._Smith_(businessman) "Richard A. Smith (businessman)") agreed to let his nephew buy the [Philadelphia Eagles](/wiki/Philadelphia_Eagles "Philadelphia Eagles"); Lurie contacted [Norman Braman](/wiki/Norman_Braman "Norman Braman"), then owner of the Eagles. Lurie bought the Philadelphia Eagles on May 6, 1994, from Braman for $195 million (equivalent to $424 million in 2025). Lurie and his mother, Nancy Lurie Marks, the only daughter of [Philip Smith](/wiki/Philip_Smith_(theater_owner) "Philip Smith (theater owner)"), borrowed $190 million from the [Bank of Boston](/wiki/Bank_of_Boston "Bank of Boston") to buy the team.[[16]](#cite_note-Encyclo-16) To back the Bank of Boston loan, Lurie put up millions of dollars' worth of personal stock in Harcourt General and GC Companies Inc. as equity.[[16]](#cite_note-Encyclo-16) He and his mother also put up stock in the family trust as collateral so Lurie could borrow the rest.[[16]](#cite_note-Encyclo-16)

In 2017, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes_(magazine) "Forbes (magazine)")* valued the Eagles at $2.65 billion, ranking them 10th among NFL teams in value.[[17]](#cite_note-17) By 2024 this valuation had increased to $6.6 billion with the Eagles ranked 8th amongst all 32 NFL teams in value.[[18]](#cite_note-18) On February 4, 2018, the Eagles defeated the Patriots to win [Super Bowl LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") by the score of 41–33, giving Lurie his first title as Eagles owner. The victory evened the score with New England, as the previous Lurie-era Super Bowl appearance was a 24–21 loss to the Patriots in [Super Bowl XXXIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXIX "Super Bowl XXXIX").[[19]](#cite_note-u266-19)

On February 9, 2025, the Eagles defeated the [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") to win [Super Bowl LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX") by the score of 40–22, giving Lurie his second title as Eagles' owner. The win evened the score against Kansas City (the Eagles had lost to the Chiefs in [Super Bowl LVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LVII "Super Bowl LVII")), and denied the Chiefs' bid to become the only team to win [three Super Bowls in a row](/wiki/Three-peat "Three-peat").[[20]](#cite_note-a252-20)

## Awards and recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jeffrey_Lurie&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Awards and recognition")]

* As owner of the Eagles, two-time [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") champion ([LII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LII "Super Bowl LII") and [LIX](/wiki/Super_Bowl_LIX "Super Bowl LIX")).[[21]](#cite_note-Fischer2025-21)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jeffrey_Lurie&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Christina-and-jeffrey-lurie-by-bill-cramer.jpg/250px-Christina-and-jeffrey-lurie-by-bill-cramer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christina-and-jeffrey-lurie-by-bill-cramer.jpg)

Christina and Jeffrey Lurie in 2010

In a pre-production meeting at his film production company,[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] working on the film, *[I Love You To Death](/wiki/I_Love_You_To_Death "I Love You To Death")* (1990), Lurie met [Christina Weiss](/wiki/Christina_Weiss_Lurie "Christina Weiss Lurie"), a former actress who working for the company.[[22]](#cite_note-FoxInquirer-22) Lurie married Weiss in [Gstaad](/wiki/Gstaad "Gstaad"), Switzerland in 1992, and they have had two children, a daughter Milena (born 1993) and a son Julian (born 1995).[[22]](#cite_note-FoxInquirer-22)

Lurie was said, by his wife Christina, to "lov[e] some of the [Jewish] holidays, like Passover", but was further described to have made no effort to provide a religious education for his children, and was otherwise described as a non-practicing Jew.[[22]](#cite_note-FoxInquirer-22)

In 2012, Christina and Jeffrey Lurie announced that they were divorcing, a process that was finalized in August 2012.[[23]](#cite_note-23) On May 4, 2013, he married again, to Tina Lai.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2020, Forbes ranked Lurie as No. 319 on the [Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes") 400 list of the richest people in America.[[25]](#cite_note-25)
